raw_case:
A case of acute eosinophilic pneumonia induced by the inhalation of acetylene
A 58-year-old man who had inhaled acetylene while welding 2 and 4 days previously and subsequently developed cough, dyspnea, and fever was referred by his general practitioner. A computed tomography scan showed diffuse ground-glass opacities in both lungs, and bronchoalveolar lavage fluid showed an increased proportion of eosinophils (25 %). Acute eosinophilic pneumonia (AEP) was diagnosed. Inhalation of acetylene was considered to be the most likely cause of AEP. Respiratory symptoms improved rapidly with intravenous corticosteroids. This is a rare case of AEP caused by inhalation of acetylene.1

Introduction

Acetylene, a commonly used industrial gas, is flammable and is mixed with oxygen for use in welding. Symptoms of acetylene inhalation include dizziness, headache, and nausea, and exposure to high concentrations of acetylene gas can be fatal [1]. Acute lung injury caused by inhalation of acetylene is rare, with only a few cases reported [2,3]. To our knowledge, only one case of acute eosinophilic pneumonia (AEP) caused by acetylene gas has been reported [3]. In this report, we present a rare case of AEP caused by inhalation of acetylene.2

Case report

We experienced a 58-year-old man who had smoked 38 pack-years since the age of 20 and had a history of allergic rhinitis, as well as of testicular cancer surgery at age 20 years. No family or psychosocial history. Three weeks before visiting our hospital, he started taking the herbal medicine hachimijiogan for frequent urination. He worked in fusion cutting for car engine parts and wore a disposable face mask at work; the ventilation in the workplace was poor. One day, the acetylene gas hose became disconnected during fusion, and the patient inhaled leaking gas. This exposure was noticed because the gas had ignited from a leaking section of another pipe. About two hours after inhalation, he began to cough and have difficulty breathing, but he did not seek medical attention. Two days later, he inhaled leaking acetylene gas again, and his dyspnea worsened. He recognized acetylene inhalation because it was accompanied by a strange odor. The second exposure was due to inadequate ventilation resulting in the inhalation of acetylene used by colleague. The next day, he developed a fever of 39 °C and went to see his local doctor. His chest x-ray showed bilateral lung consolidation, and he was referred to our hospital.On examination, he was conscious. His temperature was 37.7 °C; respiratory rate, 32/min; oxygen saturation, 97 % (with oxygen supply from a nasal cannula at a rate of 3 L/min); blood pressure, 124/70 mmHg; and pulse, 82/min. There were no significant lung sound abnormalities. Results of blood tests were as follows: white blood cell count, 8100/μL (neutrophils, 76 %; eosinophils, 9 %); lactate dehydrogenase, 569 U/L; C-reactive protein, 17.2 mg/dL; surfactant protein A, 231 ng/mL; surfactant protein D, 373 ng/mL; immunoglobulin E, 123.1 IU/mL; B-type natriuretic peptide, 86.6 pg/mL; arterial blood gas pH, 7.42; partial pressure of carbon dioxide, 37.7 mmHg; partial pressure of oxygen, 90.4 mmHg (with oxygen supply from a nasal cannula at a rate of 2 L/min); and bicarbonate, 24.1 mEq/L. A chest computed tomography (CT) scan showed central and peri-bronchial alveolar opacities with air bronchograms and consolidation pattern (Fig. 1A and B). Electrocardiogram and transthoracic echocardiography results were both considered normal. Bronchoalveolar lavage fluid (BALF) showed a total cell count of 3.00 × 103/μL, 25 % eosinophils, and 18 % neutrophils, and the patient was diagnosed with acute eosinophilic pneumonia (AEP). During bronchoscopy procedure, his respiratory status worsened, and he was intubated.Treatment was started with methylprednisolone 1000 mg/day for 3 days, ceftriaxone 2 g/day, and azithromycin 500 mg/day, after which respiratory failure rapidly improved, and the patient could be extubated on day 3. Eosinophils were almost absent after steroid pulse therapy. Prednisolone 1 mg/kg was administered from day 4 to day 7, and a chest x-ray was performed on day 1–5 (Fig. 2). On day 6, a CT scan showed almost complete improvement with only linear shadow (Fig. 1C and D). The patient was discharged on day 10. Since then, he has continued to improve.3

Discussion

AEP causes acute respiratory failure and is characterized by pulmonary eosinophilia. It is thought to be a reaction to smoking, particulate matter, and inhaled antigens. The present patient was diagnosed with AEP because he presented with acute fever, bilateral pulmonary consolidations, type 1 respiratory failure, high proportion of eosinophils in BALF (25 %), and inhaled inducer exposure [4]. A possible mechanism of gas inhalation-induced AEP is the release of interleukin (IL)-33 from the injured airway epithelium, which may induce eosinophil infiltration via the immune pathway [5]. The present patient had a history of allergic rhinitis; therefore, it is highly likely that type 2 inflammation was present in the background, which may have been one of the reasons why this type of inflammation was easily activated. In addition to elevated eosinophils in the BALF, we also observed elevated neutrophils; the inflammatory pathway associated with IL-8 stimulation is thought to be involved also in inhalant gas injury, so a similar mechanism may have occurred in the present case [5].Because acetylene gas is highly flammable, reports of industrial accidents often involve fires and explosions caused by ignition [6]. There have been reports of toxicity associated with the abuse of acetylene gas [1] and interstitial pneumonia due to acetylene gas [2]. Animal studies of acetylene inhalation showed that it causes alveolar fibrosis and necrosis by a non-immune mechanism involving oxidative and nitrosative stress, which is thought to be the pathogenesis of interstitial pneumonia [7]. AEP induced by acetylene gas are been rarely reported to date [3]. Similar to this case, accidental inhalation due to acetylene leakage has been the cause in previous cases [3], and leakage risk management is important when using acetylene gas.In the present case, lung injury due to metal fume inhalation was also considered as a possible cause of AEP. The composition of the fused car engine parts is not known; however, copper-lead alloys and aluminum alloys are often used in the production of such parts. These alloys can produce fumes, so we cannot rule out the possibility that our patient inhaled such fumes. However, because he did not experience any symptoms during normal operations without acetylene gas leakage. Drug-induced pneumonia due to hachimijiogan can also be considered as another possible cause, but in the present case the course of disease onset was clear, and AEP due to gas injury was more likely than drug-induced lung injury. In addition, hachimijiogan-induced pneumonia is reported to be rare [8]. Parasitic infection can also cause AEP, but the patient had no history of such an infection [5]. Therefore, we concluded that the acetylene gas was the major inducer of AEP. He had never been inhaled acetylene before because of adequately ventilated. It seems that the ventilation was neglected due to the cold weather. This time the Labor Standards Inspection Office gave advice, because there were problems with the management system at the workplace.4

Conclusion

We report a case of AEP induced by inhalation of acetylene. Acetylene gas should be considered as a potential cause of AEP.

CRediT authorship contribution statement

Nobuhiko Tsukada: Investigation, Conceptualization. Yusuke Nakamura: Project administration, Investigation, Conceptualization. Shohei Hara: Investigation. Arata Honda: Investigation. Azusa Tsukada: Investigation. Majima Takaaki: Investigation. Midori Tsuchiya: Investigation. Koji Wake: Investigation. Yasuo Shimizu: Supervision, Project administration, Investigation. Seiji Niho: Supervision, Project administration, Investigation.

Informed consent statement

Written informed consent was obtained from the patient for publication of this case report, including any accompanying images.

Additional information

The authors declare no competing financial interests.

IRB information

IRB review is not required.

Declaration of competing interest

The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 58-year-old male
- **Chief Complaint:** Cough, dyspnea, and fever
- **History of Present Illness:** The patient inhaled acetylene gas during welding on two occasions (2 and 4 days prior to evaluation). After the first exposure, he experienced cough and dyspnea, which worsened after the second exposure. He subsequently developed a fever of 39°C, prompting him to visit a local doctor.
- **Past Medical History:** History of allergic rhinitis and testicular cancer surgery at age 20. Smoked for 38 pack-years starting at age 20.
- **Personal History:** Works in fusion cutting for car engine parts. Reports wearing a disposable face mask with inadequate workplace ventilation. Recently started taking the herbal medicine hachimijiogan.
- **Family History:** None.
- **Physical Examination:** Conscious with a temperature of 37.7°C, respiratory rate of 32/min, oxygen saturation of 97% with oxygen support (3 L/min), blood pressure of 124/70 mmHg, and pulse of 82/min. No significant abnormalities in lung sounds.
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - Blood tests: WBC 8100/μL (neutrophils 76%, eosinophils 9%), CRP 17.2 mg/dL, LDH 569 U/L, elevated surfactant proteins A and D, IgE 123.1 IU/mL, arterial gas pH 7.42, PCO2 37.7 mmHg, PO2 90.4 mmHg (on oxygen).
  - Imaging: Chest CT showing diffuse ground-glass opacities and central peri-bronchial alveolar opacities with air bronchograms and consolidation.
  - Bronchoalveolar lavage fluid (BALF): Total cell count of 3.00 × 103/μL with 25% eosinophils and 18% neutrophils.
  - Electrocardiogram and echocardiography were normal.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
- **Step 1: Initial List of Conditions**
  - Initial differential included acute eosinophilic pneumonia (AEP), parasitic infection, drug-induced pneumonitis, metal fume-induced lung injury, and interstitial pneumonia.
  
- **Step 2: Evaluation of AEP**
  - High likelihood due to acute onset of fever, bilateral consolidation on imaging, respiratory failure, high proportion of eosinophils in BALF (25%), and exposure to a known inciting agent (acetylene). 

- **Step 3: Exclusion of Other Diagnoses**
  - **Parasitic infection:** Minimal likelihood due to lack of history of parasitic exposure.
  - **Drug-induced pneumonitis:** Considered due to recent use of hachimijiogan, but unlikely as the disease onset aligns temporally with acetylene gas inhalation. Hachimijiogan-induced AEP is rare, further lowering clinical suspicion.
  - **Metal fume-induced injury:** Less likely as the patient had no similar symptoms before acetylene exposure.
  - **Interstitial pneumonia:** Typically involves chronic processes, while the patient had an acute presentation linked to acetylene exposure.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Acute eosinophilic pneumonia (AEP) induced by inhalation of acetylene. This diagnosis is supported by the patient’s clinical presentation, exposure history, imaging findings, and the eosinophil-rich BALF results.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Acute eosinophilic pneumonia (AEP) induced by acetylene inhalation.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's start by looking at the patient's chief complaints: cough, dyspnea, and fever. He's a 58-year-old male with a history of welding and inhaling acetylene gas twice recently. After each exposure, his symptoms got worse. His past medical history includes allergic rhinitis and testicular cancer, plus he's a heavy smoker. 

First, considering the exposure to acetylene gas during welding. Acetylene itself isn't super toxic, but welding fumes can contain various harmful substances like metal oxides or other gases. Inhaling these can cause chemical pneumonitis or acute lung injury. His symptoms started after exposure, so that's a key point.

His blood tests show elevated CRP and LDH, which are markers of inflammation and possible tissue damage. The eosinophils are 9% in blood and 25% in BALF. High eosinophils often point towards an allergic or hypersensitivity reaction. The chest CT shows ground-glass opacities and consolidation, which are typical in interstitial lung diseases or pneumonia. 

He has a history of allergic rhinitis, which might predispose him to hypersensitivity reactions. The BALF with eosinophilia makes me think of eosinophilic pneumonia. There's acute eosinophilic pneumonia (AEP), which can develop rapidly after an exposure. The timing here fits—symptoms after two exposures, and the BALF showing high eosinophils. 

But also, considering the occupational exposure, could this be a form of occupational lung disease, like hypersensitivity pneumonitis? However, HP usually has a more subacute presentation and BALF typically shows lymphocytes. His BALF has more eosinophils and neutrophils, which leans more towards AEP. 

Another angle: he started taking hachimijiogan, a herbal medicine. Could this be drug-induced eosinophilic pneumonia? The timing is possible, but the stronger trigger here seems to be the acetylene exposure. Plus, the fact that symptoms started after the welding incidents makes the exposure the more likely culprit. 

His smoking history is significant. Smokers are at higher risk for certain lung conditions, but the acute presentation here is more in line with an acute injury or infection. However, the CT findings don't look like typical bacterial pneumonia, which would have more consolidation and possibly pleural effusions. The elevated surfactant proteins A and D are markers of alveolar damage, which can occur in interstitial lung diseases or acute lung injury.

The fever and elevated WBC with neutrophilia could indicate an infection, but the eosinophilia in blood and BALF suggests otherwise. Also, the lack of response to antibiotics (if he was given any) might not be mentioned here, but the case doesn't mention infection treatment.

Putting it all together: acute exposure to welding fumes leading to acute eosinophilic pneumonia. The key points are the temporal relationship between exposure and symptoms, BALF with eosinophilia, CT findings, and exclusion of other causes like infection or heart issues (normal ECG and echo). The differential could include chemical pneumonitis, but the eosinophilic component points towards AEP triggered by the inhalation injury.
results.content:
```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient’s symptoms (cough, dyspnea, fever) and timing correlate with occupational exposure to acetylene gas during welding, suggesting an inhalational injury. The rapid worsening after repeated exposure supports an acute reaction to inhaled toxins or irritants.

<step 2> Blood tests reveal neutrophilia and marked eosinophilia (9% in blood, 25% in BALF), elevated CRP, LDH, and surfactant proteins. These findings indicate significant inflammation and alveolar damage, with eosinophilic infiltration pointing to a hypersensitivity or immune-mediated process.

<step 3> Chest CT findings (diffuse ground-glass opacities, consolidation with air bronchograms) and BALF results align with eosinophilic pneumonia. The absence of infection markers (e.g., no mention of sputum cultures or elevated procalcitonin) and normal cardiac studies (ECG, echocardiogram) exclude typical pneumonia or heart failure.

<step 4> Acute eosinophilic pneumonia (AEP) is strongly supported by the rapid onset post-exposure, BALF eosinophilia (>25%), and imaging findings. While acetylene gas itself is not highly toxic, welding fumes often contain metal oxides or other irritants that can trigger AEP, especially in a patient with allergic rhinitis (a predisposing factor for hypersensitivity reactions).

<step 5> Alternative diagnoses, such as chemical pneumonitis or hypersensitivity pneumonitis, are less likely. Chemical pneumonitis typically lacks significant eosinophilia, while hypersensitivity pneumonitis usually presents with lymphocytosis in BALF and subacute/chronic symptoms. Drug-induced lung injury from hachimijiogan is possible but less temporally correlated than the welding exposures.

### Answer:
Acute eosinophilic pneumonia secondary to welding fume exposure.

reasoning_eval.step: The patient’s symptoms (cough, dyspnea, fever) and timing correlate with occupational exposure to acetylene gas during welding, suggesting an inhalational injury. The rapid worsening after repeated exposure supports an acute reaction to inhaled toxins or irritants.

Blood tests reveal neutrophilia and marked eosinophilia (9% in blood, 25% in BALF), elevated CRP, LDH, and surfactant proteins. These findings indicate significant inflammation and alveolar damage, with eosinophilic infiltration pointing to a hypersensitivity or immune-mediated process.

Chest CT findings (diffuse ground-glass opacities, consolidation with air bronchograms) and BALF results align with eosinophilic pneumonia. The absence of infection markers (e.g., no mention of sputum cultures or elevated procalcitonin) and normal cardiac studies (ECG, echocardiogram) exclude typical pneumonia or heart failure.

Acute eosinophilic pneumonia (AEP) is strongly supported by the rapid onset post-exposure, BALF eosinophilia (>25%), and imaging findings. While acetylene gas itself is not highly toxic, welding fumes often contain metal oxides or other irritants that can trigger AEP, especially in a patient with allergic rhinitis (a predisposing factor for hypersensitivity reactions).

Alternative diagnoses, such as chemical pneumonitis or hypersensitivity pneumonitis, are less likely. Chemical pneumonitis typically lacks significant eosinophilia, while hypersensitivity pneumonitis usually presents with lymphocytosis in BALF and subacute/chronic symptoms. Drug-induced lung injury from hachimijiogan is possible but less temporally correlated than the welding exposures.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Initial differential included acute eosinophilic pneumonia (AEP), parasitic infection, drug-induced pneumonitis, metal fume-induced lung injury, and interstitial pneumonia.
High likelihood of AEP due to acute onset of fever, bilateral consolidation on imaging, respiratory failure, high proportion of eosinophils in BALF (25%), and exposure to acetylene.
Minimal likelihood of parasitic infection due to lack of parasitic exposure history.
Considered drug-induced pneumonitis from hachimijiogan, but unlikely as disease onset aligned with acetylene exposure; hachimijiogan-induced AEP is rare.
Less likely metal fume-induced injury as patient had no similar symptoms before acetylene exposure.
Less likely interstitial pneumonia due to acute presentation linked to acetylene exposure.
Final diagnosis: Acute eosinophilic pneumonia (AEP) induced by inhalation of acetylene, supported by clinical presentation, exposure history, imaging findings, and eosinophil-rich BALF results.
